images.caption: Figure 1:Example outputs from LLaDA with standard sampling (left) and our approach (right), both with a temperature of 1. For this example, the base sampler achieved a Pass@16 of 0, while our model found 3 valid solutions. Despite having the same temperature, standard sampling leads to mode collapse over an incorrect approach, whereas our method optimises to select samples distinct from those previously explored.

Figure 2:An overview of our proposed diverse sampling framework, using our orthogonal diversity loss. Given a full batch ofnnsamples, feature vectorsF​(𝐱𝐢)F(\mathbf{x_{i}})are extracted for the logitsxix_{i}for each samplei∈{1,…,n}i\in\{1,\dots,n\}.
We then loop through each sample, compute an orthogonal basis for all previous sample featuresB<iB_{<i}, and calculate the projection of the current sample features onto this basis. We then take the norm of the residual with respect to this projection as the diversity objective. Optimising this then moves the sample in a direction diverse compared to previous samples.

Figure 3:Average batch diversity (1 - cosine similarity of sentence embeddings) for GSM8K (top) and HumanEval (bottom), comparing ODD to the baseline (α=0\alpha=0, as indicated by dashed lines). ODD increases diversity at low temperatures and acts as a coherence filter at high temperatures (evidenced by the improved Pass@16 in Table1), effectively balancing exploration and quality.

Figure 4:Pass@1 vs Pass@16 Pareto frontiers.α=0\alpha=0represents standard LLaDA sampling (baseline). Data points represent decreasing temperatures from right (θ=0\theta=0) to left (θ=2\theta=2).Top (GSM8K):ODD trades off individual accuracy (shifting left) for superior batch coverage (shifting up).Bottom (HumanEval):Forα≤16\alpha\leq 16, ODD achieves a Pareto improvement, boosting coverage without loss of quality.

Figure 5:Empirical Pass@kkfor ODD over GSM8K (top) and HumanEval (bottom). We observe a consistent improvement relative to baseline for ODD. Given the batch size invariance of ODD, we expect this trend would continue for higher batch sizes.

Figure 6:Computational overhead of ODD compared to standard sampling.Top:Global averages across all runs.Bottom:Scaling behavior demonstrating time and memory overheads across batch size (BB), sequence length (SS), and diffusion steps (TT). ODD incurs less than a10%10\%relative time penalty, with memory scaling independently of the base model size.
